coredata.dc:title: Energy transfer mediated rapid and visual discrimination of tetracyclines and quercetin in food by using N, Cu Co-doped carbon dots
coredata.dc:creator: Li, Tingting
Guo, Guoqiang
Xing, Haoming
Wang, Yiru
Luo, Xinyu
Wang, Linfan
Gu, Chenjie
Hou, Yafei
Chen, Da
coredata.dc:description: The appearance of multi-drug resistant Escherichia coli makes the combination of tetracyclines (TCs) and quercetin (QCT) more common to fight stubborn bacterial infections so that the effective detections of TCs and QCT are essential and necessary. Here, a novel fluorescence probe for differentiating TCs and QCT is developed based on the nitrogen and copper co-doped carbon dots (N, Cu-CDs). The N, Cu-CDs are prepared from ethylene diamine tetraacetic acid (EDTA) and anhydrous copper chloride as precursors through hydrothermal process and exhibit bright blue fluorescence with excellent optical stability. With the presence of four tetracyclines (DOX, TC, CTC and OTC), the fluorescence intensity of N, Cu-CDs is quenched directly due to the internal filtration effect (IFE), and the detection limit obtained through single-signal fluorescence sensing is as low as 23.8 nM for DOX, 37.2 nM for TC, 43.8 nM for OTC and 28.8 nM for CTC. More remarkably, three dimensional ratiometric fluorescence probe for detecting QCT is proposed based on the appearance of another emission at (410 nm, 490 nm) due to electron transform (ET) process. This new method shows a good linear relationship in the range of 10–100 μM with a low detection limit of 59.3 nM. Furthermore, a dual-channel fluorescence sensing platform based on microfluidics paper-based analytical devices (μPADs) is developed for simultaneously visual discrimination of TCs (DOX is chosen as the typical detecting model for TCs) and QCT. This investigation provides a new way for the development of CDs as multifunction fluorescence probes.
coredata.dcterms:subject: N
Cu-doped carbon dots
Doxycycline
Quercetin
3D ratiometric fluorescence probe
Visual discrimination
Microfluidics paper-based analytical devices

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Fluorescence detection of TCs and QCT
Simultaneous detection of DOX and QCT by μPADs
Optical properties of the N, Cu-CDs
Dual-channel fluorescence sensing platform based on μPADs

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency: K C Wong Magna Fund in Ningbo University

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency-matched-string: National Natural Science Foundation of China
xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency: National Natural Science Foundation of China

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: This work was supported by projects from the National Natural Science Foundation of China under Grant (No. 61604084 ), the K. C. Wong Magna Fund in Ningbo University . The authors declare no competing ﬁnancial interest.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Schematic diagram of the preparation process and applications of N, Cu-CDs.

Characterization of N, Cu-CDs. (a) TEM image of N, Cu-CDs (Inset shows the HR-TEM image). (b) The size distribution of N, Cu-CDs. (c) AFM image of N, Cu-CDs. (d) Raman spectrum of N, Cu-CDs. (e) FT-IR spectra of N, Cu-CDs (blank line) and EDTA (red line). (f) XPS spectrum of N, Cu-CDs. High-resolution XPS spectrum of C 1s (g), N 1s (h) and Cu 2p (i). (For interpretation of the references to color in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the Web version of this article.)

Optical properties of N, Cu-CDs. (a) UV–vis absorption, PLE and PL spectra of N, Cu-CDs. The inset presents the optical images of N, Cu-CDs aqueous solution under sunlight (left) and a 365 nm illumination (right). (b) 3D fluorescent matric scanning map of N, Cu-CDs. The fluorescence stability of N, Cu-CDs under different pH values (c) and storage time in daylight (d).

(a) The selectivity of N, Cu-CDs to various metal ions, amino acid, TCs and QCT. (b) Different responses of N, Cu-CDs towards TCs and QCT. (c) Fluorescence spectra of N, Cu-CDs with different concentrations of DOX. (d) The relationship between fluorescence intensity ratio (F/F0) and the concentration of DOX. The inset is the images of N, Cu-CDs with different concentrations of DOX under sunlight and a UV light. (e) The linear fitting between F/F0 and the concentration of DOX. (f–i) 3D fluorescent matric scanning maps of N, Cu-CDs with different concentration of QCT. (j) The relationship between fluorescence intensity ratio (IFA-1/IFA-2) and the concentration of QCT. The inset is images of N, Cu-CDs with different concentration of QCT at the excitation wavelength of 365 nm and 410 nm. (k) The linear fitting between IFA-1/IFA-2 and the concentration of QCT.

(a) Fluorescence intensity of FA-2 and QCT with different QCT concentration. (b) FT-IR spectra of N, Cu-CDs in the presence of QCT. (c) High-resolution XPS spectrum of Cu 2p of N, Cu-CDs in the presence of QCT. (d) UV–vis absorption spectra of N, Cu-CDs with different QCT concentrations. (e) The UV–vis absorption spectrum of QCT, PLE and PL spectra of N, Cu-CDs. Schematic energy level diagram and the ET process in the presence of QCT: FA-1 (f), FA-2 (g). The time-resolved PL spectra of N, Cu-CDs in the presence and absence of QCT monitored at the excitation wavelength of 340 nm (h) and 450 nm (i).

Hardware design of dual-channel paper-based fluorescence sensing platform: (a) 3D structure design, (b) optical design and (c) μPADs. (d) The images and fluorescence spectra of μPADs with different DOX/QCT concentrations. (e) The relationship between B values and the concentration of DOX. (f) The relationship between the ratio value of B/G and the concentration of QCT. (g) The images and fluorescence spectra of μPADs in real samples.

head.ce:title: Energy transfer mediated rapid and visual discrimination of tetracyclines and quercetin in food by using N, Cu Co-doped carbon dots

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Microelectronic Science and Engineering, School of Physical Science and Technology, Ningbo University, Ningbo, 315211, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Department of Microelectronic Science and Engineering
School of Physical Science and Technology

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Department of Microelectronic Science and Engineering, School of Physical Science and Technology, Ningbo University, Ningbo, 315211, P.R. China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: The appearance of multi-drug resistant Escherichia coli makes the combination of tetracyclines (TCs) and quercetin (QCT) more common to fight stubborn bacterial infections so that the effective detections of TCs and QCT are essential and necessary. Here, a novel fluorescence probe for differentiating TCs and QCT is developed based on the nitrogen and copper co-doped carbon dots (N, Cu-CDs). The N, Cu-CDs are prepared from ethylene diamine tetraacetic acid (EDTA) and anhydrous copper chloride as precursors through hydrothermal process and exhibit bright blue fluorescence with excellent optical stability. With the presence of four tetracyclines (DOX, TC, CTC and OTC), the fluorescence intensity of N, Cu-CDs is quenched directly due to the internal filtration effect (IFE), and the detection limit obtained through single-signal fluorescence sensing is as low as 23.8 nM for DOX, 37.2 nM for TC, 43.8 nM for OTC and 28.8 nM for CTC. More remarkably, three dimensional ratiometric fluorescence probe for detecting QCT is proposed based on the appearance of another emission at (410 nm, 490 nm) due to electron transform (ET) process. This new method shows a good linear relationship in the range of 10–100 μM with a low detection limit of 59.3 nM. Furthermore, a dual-channel fluorescence sensing platform based on microfluidics paper-based analytical devices (μPADs) is developed for simultaneously visual discrimination of TCs (DOX is chosen as the typical detecting model for TCs) and QCT. This investigation provides a new way for the development of CDs as multifunction fluorescence probes.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: As an essential symbiont of the mammalian human gut, Escherichia coli (E. coli) provides a source of vitamin K and other essential compounds, but it can also cause gastrointestinal tract infection or urethral infection in human and various animals under certain conditions [1]. Typically, tetracyclines (TCs), mainly including tetracycline (TC), chlortetracycline (CTC), oxytetracycline (OTC) and doxycycline (DOX), play an important role in reducing the morbidity and mortality related to bacterial illnesses in animals and humans. As a kind of broad-spectrum antibiotics produced by actinomycetes, TCs mainly restrain the synthesis of the bacterial protein [2]. Due to its low prices, high qualities and excellent production purity, TCs have been widely applied to treatments of infectious diseases in humans and animals [3], growth-promoting additives in animal fodder [4] and so forth. However, serious ecological problems induced by the abuse of TCs leave vast residues in the environment [5–7]. More seriously, TCs abuse and overuse have led to the emergence of resistant bacteria and enabled the transmission of antibiotic resistance genes [8]. In recent years, the antibiotic resistant strains of E. coli have emerged and can easily incorporate into the intestinal microbiome, which greatly reduce the effectiveness of TCs [9–11].

Surprisingly, recent studies show that quercetin (QCT) possesses inherent antibacterial properties against E. coli and can cooperate with TCs to obtain greater antibacterial effects [12–14]. QCT, as a polyphenolic flavonoid compound, is a kind of secondary metabolites of plants, which could be abundantly found in fruits, vegetables, seeds, tea and other natural plants [15]. Previous studies have reported that QCT has high medical values, including protection against with cancers, tumors, pulmonary and cardiovascular diseases [16]. More importantly, the combination of conventional TCs and QCT is more effective against multi-drug resistant E. coli. Until now, this dual-antibiotic treatment has become more popular in the treatment of stubborn bacterial infections [12,13]. However, with the further application of this strategy, more and more TCs residues enter groundwater and soil in the environment and are taken up by various plants, crops and herbivorous animals [17]. A large number of TCs are bioaccumulated by people from crops and animal-derived food through the food chain, posing potential harm to human health, such as tolerance, hearing loss, inhibition of bone marrow hematopoietic function caused by aplastic anemia and granular leukocyte deficiency diseases [18,19]. Meanwhile, excessive consumption of QCT can regulate certain transcription factors or key proteins involved in cell signaling pathways and expression of functional genes, resulting in affecting metabolic characteristics [20]. Thus, it is of great significance for human health and environmental preservation to explore a novel and dual-function sensor to accurately detect TCs and QCT at the same time.

Carbon dots (CDs), as a novel fluorescence material, have drawn increasing attention due to their excellent properties, such as the superior merits of optical peculiarities [21], favorable stability [22], low toxicity [23], good biocompatibility [24] and brief building-up process [14]. Due to these advantages, CDs are regarded as a promising fluorescence material and have been widely used in bio-imaging, catalysis, light-emitting diode fields and so on [25]. More importantly, fluorescence probes based on CDs for detecting TCs or QCT are proposed in recent years because of their excellent properties and bright prospects. For instance, Cheng et al. have reported a fluorescent probe for QCT detection based on inner filter effect (IFE) [26]. Wang et al. have realized the detection of DOX by using fluorescent CDs prepared from phthalic acid and ethylene diamine [27]. Yu et al. have synthesized CDs as a sensing platform for highly sensitive sensing QCT [28]. However, the use of CDs for simultaneously detecting and discriminating TCs and QCT has received less attention. It is noted that nitrogen doping has been widely used for improving the quantum yield of CDs, and the related N-doped CDs have been applied to achieve the determination of TCs [7]. In addition, the carboxylic group of CDs has a high affinity with Cu2+ ions. Meanwhile, QCT is an effective metal chelator, which can coordinate with the Cu2+ center to form stable complexes as well as to control the aggregation behavior. This gives a great impetus to develop a novel and dual-function fluorescence sensor for the detection of TCs and QCT by nitrogen and copper co-doped CDs (N, Cu-CDs). In addition, due to the uncontrollable experimental conditions, such as the matrix background and instrument efficiency, the ratiometric fluorescent sensing method has been emphasized as necessary, in which the sensing analyte can differently affect the emission peak intensities of a dual- (or multi-) emitting probe. By comparison, the ratiometric detection based on the three-dimensional (3D) fluorescent scanning map integrates more comprehensive fluorescence information and can realize higher precision of target detection. Hence, it is very necessary to construct a feasible route of ratiometric fluorescent probe to accurately detect TCs and QCT at the same time.

Enlightened by the requirement of effective and rapid detection, a sensitive fluorescent probe is illustrated in Fig. 1 . The N, Cu-CDs, obtained by using ethylene diamine tetraacetic acid (EDTA) and anhydrous copper chloride (CuCl2) as precursor with hydrothermal method, achieve multi-target detection with excellent sensitivity to TCs and QCT. Here, doxycycline (DOX) is chosen as the typical detecting model for TCs. The fluorescence intensity of N, Cu-CDs is quenched in the presence of DOX due to IFE and the limit of detection (LOD) is 23.8 nM calculated through the single intensity-based fluorescence sensing method. Meanwhile, the 3D fluorescent ratiometric detection method is proposed to realize quantitative determination of QCT. Upon the addition of QCT, besides the quenched emission peak (362 nm, 432 nm) in blue fluorescence region, another emission center (410 nm, 490 nm) is quenching at a slower speed. This 3D ratiometric detection method shows an excellent linear regression in the concentration range from 10 to 100 μM with the detection limit of 59.3 nM. Simultaneously, a novel kind of dual-channel paper-based fluorescence sensing platform based on microfluidics paper-based analytical devices (μPADs) is designed for rapid discrimination and visual detection towards DOX and QCT. Thus, this work not only provides a new sensing method, but also expands the application of N, Cu-CDs in the multi-target sensing probe.

All chemical reagents were purchased as analytical grade without any further purification. EDTA, DOX, oxytetracycline (OTC), chlorotetracycline (CTC), tetracycline (TC), QCT, NaCl, KCl, AgCl, CuCl2, HgCl2, MgCl2, CaCl2, PbCl2, FeCl3, AlCl3, l-Cysteine (Cys), alanine (Ala), proline (Pro), serine (Ser), l-Asparagine (Asn), tryptophan (Trp), Leucine (Leu), ethanol (≥99.8%), NaOH (≥96.0%), H2SO4 (≥96.0%) and other chemicals were purchases from Aladdin (Shanghai, China). The ultrapure water used during all the experiments was obtained from the Millipore system.

The hydrothermal treatment was adopted for the preparation of N, Cu-CDs. Typically, 3 mmol of EDTA and 1 mmol of CuCl2 were dissolved in 25 mL of deionized water with ultrasonic treatment. Then, the mixture solution was transferred to a 100 mL Teflon lined stainless steel autoclave and heated at 200 °C for 8 h. After the sample was cooled down to room temperature, the obtained solution was filtered through a 0.22 μm filter to remove large particles. Then, the solution was further purified through a dialysis membrane (500 Da) for 48 h. Finally, the purified N, Cu-CDs were preserved at 4 °C for the further experiments.

Morphologies of the N, Cu-CDs were characterized by using transmission electron microscope (TEM, JEM-2100F, Japan Electron) and atomic force microscope (AFM, Cypher S, Oxford Instruments). Fourier transform infrared spectroscopy (FT-IR) was performed on a Nexus 470 (Thermo Fisher) spectrometer. The X-ray diffraction (XRD) pattern was performed by X-ray diffraction (PANalytical X'Pert Pro MPD). X-ray photoelectron spectra (XPS, Ulvac-PHI) of N, Cu-CDs were obtained with a PHI1580EXCA photoelectron spectrometer. The ultraviolet–visible (UV–vis) absorption properties of N, Cu-CDs were characterized by a UV-5800 spectrophotometer. Photoluminescence (PL) and photoluminescence excitation (PLE) spectra were collected by a PerkinElmer LS55 luminescence spectrometer (PerkinElmer Instruments, U.K.) at room temperature. A SCIENTZ-100F freeze dryer was used to obtain N, Cu-CDs solid powder.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Fluorescence detection of TCs and QCT
ce:section.ce:para: The N, Cu-CDs solution was added to a series of solution containing various concentrations of DOX. After 30 s of incubation at room temperature with well mixing, the fluorescence emission spectra were recorded at room temperature under the excitation wavelength of 362 nm. The selectivity of the probe for TCs was measured by introducing various kinds of ions, amino acid and small organic molecules with the same condition. Similarly, the standard QCT solutions with different concentrations were prepared. Subsequently, the obtained QCT solutions containing different concentrations from 10 to 1000 μM was mixed with the N, Cu-CDs solution, respectively. The resultant solutions were incubated at room temperature under well-mixing conditions. After mixed evenly, the change in 3D fluorescence maps was recorded.

Non-fluorescent rice paper with a square of 48 mm × 17 mm size was selected as the substrate to fabricate the μPADs. The molds with specific pattern and size were placed on the paper, and then a commercially obtained black oil-based marker was selected to directly plot the pattern of the mold to form a hydrophobic barrier on the paper. The device consists of three components: (i) central sample zone, (ii) test zones, and (iii) I-shaped path. The central sample zone and two circular test zones were manufactured with a diameter of 11 mm. In addition, the I-shaped path were designed to connect sample zone and test zone with the length and width of 8 mm and 7 mm, respectively.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Simultaneous detection of DOX and QCT by μPADs
ce:section.ce:para: DOX and QCT standard solutions with different concentrations or real samples were added dropwise to the sample zone of μPADs and placed in the indoor environment for incubation 3 min. After full penetration, the μPADs was inserted into the dual-channel fluorescence sensing platform and the fluorescence images can be observed through the text window. The software installed on the smartphone called “Color recognition” can control smartphone to collect photo images and output the corresponding RGB value of the fluorescence images. DOX was quantitatively analyzed based on the B value and QCT detection was based on the ratio of B/G value.

The practicability of the fluorescent sensor and the paper strip in liquid samples was demonstrated by the recovery rate of the standard addition experiment. Meat leachate, milk, tap water and waste water were chosen as real samples and detected by the developed fluorescent sensing platform. 5 g of pork was placed into 100 mL centrifuge tube and 50 mL of water was added into the tube. After mixing well, the leachate was obtained. The milk was bought from local supermarket near Ningbo University. The waste water was collected from the campus of Ningbo University. All liquid samples (100 mL of meat leachate, milk, tap water and waste water) were filtered by 0.22 μm Millipore filter to remove the solid impurity. Subsequently, the liquid samples after treatment were spiked with the different levels of DOX or QCT and well mixed for later detection. Meanwhile, high performance liquid chromatography (HPLC) was used as a reference method, and the corresponding result of t-test was analyzed.

The morphology and structure information of the prepared N, Cu-CDs are analyzed by TEM and AFM. As shown in Fig. 2 (a), the N, Cu-CDs are uniform in size and form monodisperse spheres. The high-resolution TEM (HRTEM) image (inset in Fig. 2(a)) displays a lattice fringe spacing of 0.24 nm, which corresponds to the (100) plane of sp2 graphitic carbon [29]. The size distribution in Fig. 2(b) shows that the synthesized N, Cu-CDs mainly range from 1.38 nm to 8.69 nm with an average size of 5.06 nm in the selected area. The AFM image (Fig. 2(c)) reveals that N, Cu-CDs are monodispersed with height of approximately 1.2 nm. The XRD pattern (Fig. S1) clearly indicates that N, Cu-CDs have two broad humps at about 26° and 43°, which could be indexed by the (002) and (100) diffraction peaks of carbon-based materials respectively [30]. The Raman spectrum of the N, Cu-CDs is recorded in Fig. 2(d). It is obvious that there are two peaks at 1380 cm−1 (D) and 1530 cm−1 (G). As an indicator of the defect density of crystalline structure, the ID/IG of N, Cu-CDs is 0.86. The Fourier transform infrared (FT-IR) spectrum of N, Cu-CDs (Fig. 2(e)) shows the broad absorption band at 3250-3426 cm−1, which is attributed to the stretching vibrations of O–H/N–H [31,32]. This band is much higher than that of precursor, imparting the excellent water-solubility of N, Cu-CDs. The peak located at 2832 cm−1 can be ascribed to the stretching vibrations of C–H and the peak centered at 1738 cm−1 stems from CO/CN stretching vibrations [33,34]. The stretching vibrations of CC and C–N appear at around 1443 cm−1 and 1347 cm−1, respectively [35]. It can be seen that the N, Cu-CDs show similar characteristic absorption bands with EDTA, such as CC, CO and C–N, demonstrating EDTA has been carbonized as the main part of N, Cu-CDs. More importantly, the appearance of peak from 900 cm−1 to 1100 cm−1 in the FT-IR spectrum of N, Cu-CDs is further identified as the N–Cu–N stretching vibration and the peak at 613 cm−1 is ascribed to the Cu–O vibrations, indicating the successful synthesis of Cu-containing complex in N, Cu-CDs [36].

To further confirm the elements and the composition of the N, Cu-CDs, the X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) is performed. The full XPS spectrum of N, Cu-CDs exhibits four clear peaks at 285.1 eV, 401.2 eV, 532.1 eV and 935.1 eV (Fig. 2(f)), which are attributed to C 1s, N 1s, O 1s and Cu 2p with the atomic concentration percentage of 67.09%, 13.42%, 17.56% and 1.93% (Fig. S2), respectively. In Fig. 2(g), the high-resolution XPS spectrum of C 1s band can be divided into three peaks at 284.1 eV, 286.1 eV and 287.5 eV, which are attributed to CC/C–C, CN and CO, respectively [37]. The appearances of the N 1s peaks (Fig. 2(h)) at 399.9 eV and 401.7 eV indicate the existence of C–N and CN, which is consistent with the result of FT-IR. The O 1s spectrum (Fig. S3) consisting of three peaks at 531.5 eV, 532.1 eV and 533.4 eV, can be assigned to C–O, CO/NO and Cu–O, respectively [5]. More importantly, the detailed Cu 2p spectrum (Fig. 2(i)) shows peaks at 932.4 eV, 933.7 eV and 952.6 eV with corresponding to the Cu 2p3/2, Cu–O and Cu 2p1/2 bonding orbitals, demonstrating the existence of Cu atoms in the as-prepared CDs [36].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Optical properties of the N, Cu-CDs
ce:section.ce:para: Optical properties of the N, Cu-CDs are evaluated through the UV–vis absorption, PLE and PL spectra at room temperature. As shown in Fig. 3 (a), the as-prepared N, Cu-CDs possess the absorption peak at 243 nm and 347 nm, which originate from the π-π* transition of CC and the n-π* transition of CO [37]. Under the optimal excitation wavelength (λex = 362 nm), the N, Cu-CDs aqueous solution exhibits blue emission peak at 432 nm. The N, Cu-CDs solution is transparent in visible light and displays a strong blue light under 365 nm UV light irradiation. In addition, Fig. 3(b) reveals a more intuitive view of the relationship between emission and excitation wavelengths of N, Cu-CDs by three-dimensional map, and a small fluorescence area (λex = 362 nm, λem = 432 nm) is displayed, which exhibits the excitation-independent fluorescence emission of N, Cu-CDs.

Furthermore, the optical stability of N, Cu-CDs is investigated at different conditions. As shown in Fig. 3(c), the change of N, Cu-CDs fluorescence intensity is tiny and can be negligible from pH = 2 to pH = 11, showing the superior stability at different pH conditions. When the pH value reaches to 12, the fluorescence intensity of N, Cu-CDs is quenched rapidly because of the electrostatic interaction between OH- and copper ions on the surface of N, Cu-CDs [38]. The long-term storage stability up to 20 days is demonstrated in Fig. 3(d). Besides, the superior fluorescence stability of N, Cu-CDs is presented even under ultraviolet light for 12 h or the concentration of NaCl up to 2 mol/L (Fig. S4).

Because of the good fluorescence properties and excellent photostability of N, Cu-CDs, we further explored the application of N, Cu-CDs in the field of fluorescence sensors. As displayed in Fig. 4 (a), the fluorescence intensity of N, Cu-CDs can only be selectively quenched by TCs (TC, OTC, CTC and DOX) and QCT. More importantly, the addition of TCs or QCT in N, Cu-CDs solution will produce different reactions, namely the direct quenching of fluorescence or the appearance of double fluorescence region (Fig. 4(b)). These results demonstrate that the N, Cu-CDs have good specificity for the determination of TCs and QCT. Here, DOX is chosen as the representative analyte for TCs. As indicated in Fig. 4(c), upon increasing the DOX concentration, the PL intensity of N, Cu-CDs solution decreases gradually. Fig. 4(d) shows that F/F0 decreases with the increase of DOX concentration, and the inset shows the corresponding photos of N, Cu-CDs solution with different concentration of DOX under sunlight and ultraviolet light. A good linear correlation between the F/F0 ratio of N, Cu-CDs and the DOX concentration in the range of 5–100 μM is obtained in Fig. 4(e), giving a linear regression equation of F/F0 = −0.0044CDOX+0.9496 (R2 = 0.9771), where F and F0 are the fluorescence intensity of N, Cu-CDs with and without the addition of DOX and the CDOX is the concentration of the fluorescence DOX (μM·L−1). The calculated LOD is as low as 23.8 nM. Similarly, the corresponding LODs of TC, OTC and CTC calculated by the same method are 37.2 nM, 43.8 nM and 28.8 nM (Fig. S5), respectively.

The quenching mechanism of N, Cu-CDs toward DOX is explored by the UV–vis absorption, PLE and PL spectra and the PL decay lifetimes. Compared with the pure N, Cu-CDs solution, there is no newly appeared absorption peak after the addition of DOX, illustrating that no complexes form (Fig. S6(a)) [39]. In addition, Fig. S6(b) shows that the DOX has a large overlap with the excitation peak and a small overlap with the emission peak of N, Cu-CDs, which is assumed that the fluorescence quenching mechanism may be due to the IFE and fluorescence resonance energy transfer (FRET). To further confirm the mechanism, the average fluorescence lifetimes of the N, Cu-CDs in the presence and absence of DOX are determined to be 4.41 ns and 4.49 ns (Fig. S6(c)), respectively, indicating no energy transfer process occurs between N, Cu-CDs and DOX. Therefore, it is believed that the IFE dominates the fluorescence quenching process. Similarly, due to the similar absorption characteristics, the large overlap is also observed between the UV–vis absorption spectra of the other TCs (TC, OTC and CTC) and the excitation spectrum of N, Cu-CDs (Fig. S6(d-f)), indicating the same quenching mechanism in other detection of TCs.

Different with the direct quenching of a single peak, there are two excitation peaks gradually appearing during the detection of QCT in N, Cu-CDs solution (Fig. S7). To acquire more comprehensive fluorescence information, 3D fluorescent matric scanning maps are chosen to record the fluorescence responses of N, Cu-CDs towards QCT. For the N, Cu-CDs solution, as shown in Fig. 4(f), there is a fluorescence emission center (FA-1) with excitation wavelength ranging from 330 to 450 nm, which is consistent with the excitation-independent fluorescence emission. With the increase of QCT concentration, a new fluorescence emission center (FA-2) gradually appears and has similar attenuation of fluorescence intensity with FA-1 (Fig. 4(g)). When the concentration of QCT reaches 200 μM, both of the fluorescence peaks are quenched continuously, and the intensity of FA-2 is higher apparently than that of FA-1 (Fig. 4(h)). Finally, the fluorescence intensity of FA-1 is almost quenched completely and FA-2 is still existent (Fig. 4(i)). Based on the normalized 3D fluorescent matric scanning maps of N, Cu-CDs with different concentration of QCT (Fig. S8), the optimum emission and excitation wavelengths and the corresponding intensities are extracted in Fig. S9. Therefore, a novel ratiometric fluorescent probe for QCT detection is proposed. Fig. 4(j) shows the relationship between the fluorescence intensity ratio (IFA-1/IFA-2) from 3D fluorescent matric scanning map of N, Cu-CDs and the concentration of QCT in the range from 10 to 1000 μM. The inset is the images of N, Cu-CDs solution with different concentration of QCT at the excitation wavelength of 365 nm and 410 nm. A good linear correlation between the IFA-1/IFA-2 and the QCT concentration in the range of 10–100 μM is obtained in Fig. 4(k), which can be well described by the Stern-Volmer equation of IFA-1/IFA-2 = −0.0043CQCT+1.3915, where IFA-1 and IFA-2 represent the intensity of FA-1 and FA-2, respectively, and CQCT is the concentration of QCT. The detection limit is calculated to be 59.3 nM by using the slope determined based on 3σ/m, where σ is the standard deviation of 10 blank measurements and m is the slope of the calibration line.

To clarify the sensing mechanism of N, Cu-CDs towards QCT, the source of FA-2 is further studied. As shown in Fig. S10, the emission center position of QCT with the excitation wavelength ranging from 400 nm to 470 nm is almost consistent with that of FA-2, which demonstrates the origin of FA-2 may be ascribed to the QCT-related fluorophores formed during the detection. However, different with the low fluorescence yield of QCT, it is surprising that FA-2 exhibits higher fluorescence intensity. In addition, as the QCT content increases, the fluorescence intensity of FA-2 gradually reduces, presenting a different tendency from the increase of fluorescence intensity of QCT alone (Fig. 5 (a)). These results reveal the change of the electron cloud density of QCT-related fluorophores. Previous studies have reported that QCT can form various complexes with different metal ions, including Cu2+, Al3+, Fe2+, Cd2+ and so on, which make it possible for the electron transfer (ET) process between N, Cu-CDs and QCT 40,41]. For example, Wang et al. developed a fluorescence ratiometric sensing system between red-emission CDs and QCT-Al3+ [40]. Therefore, it is reasonable that N, Cu-CDs and QCT can be considered as elector donor and accepter respectively, and the ET process will be established within them. As presented in Fig. 5(b), the CO peak position of N, Cu-CDs moves from 1738 cm−1 to around 1745 cm−1 and the absorption band of Cu–O is much higher after adding QCT, which is attributed to the chelation of QCT with N, Cu-CDs [41]. The change in high-resolution XPS spectrum of Cu 2p is presented in Fig. 5(c), where the content of Cu–O increases obviously from 4.9% to 15.1% after adding QCT (Fig. S11), which further confirms the interaction between N, Cu-CDs and QCT. Moreover, the UV–vis absorption spectra of N, Cu-CDs exhibit a significant change with QCT increasing. As shown in Fig. 5(d), the red shift of the absorption peak at 243 nm shows the change of the electron cloud density in the sp2 core, which can be ascribed to the different complexes formed by QCT and N, Cu-CDs (Fig. S12) [42].

Subsequently, the quenching mechanisms of two fluorescence areas are elucidated by using UV–vis absorption, PLE and PL spectra. As shown in Fig. 5(e), the overlapping areas between the absorption spectrum of QCT and the excitation and emission spectra of N, Cu-CDs indicate that IFE and FRET processes occur between them, resulting in the less emission energy of FA-1. In addition, the interaction between N, Cu-CDs and QCT provides the possible channel for ET progress. The excited electrons are transferred from N, Cu-CDs to QCT followed by radiation recombination during the detection, which leads to the appearance of FA-2. Consequently, the electron transition in N, Cu-CDs is extremely restrained, resulting in fluorescence quenching of FA-1 (Fig. 5(f)). Furthermore, under high excitation wavelength, the inefficient excitation energy and insufficient ET progress cause the fluorescence quenching of FA-2 (Fig. 5(g)). The fluorescence quenching mechanism of the two emission centers is shown in Fig. S13. To further confirm the sensing mechanism, the fluorescence attenuation curve of N, Cu-CDs in the presence and absence of QCT is investigated at the excitation wavelength of 340 nm in Fig. 5(h). The fluorescence decay traces are well fitted by the following double-exponential equation:
(2)
I
( t )
=
C 1
exp
(
− t / τ
1
)
+
C 2
exp
(
− t / τ
2
)
where τ1 and τ2 are decay time constant, and C1 and C2 are the ratios of time resolved decay lifetime that τ1 and τ2 take. It is clearly shown that fluorescence lifetime of N, Cu-CDs exhibits double exponential attenuation: fast decay coefficient (τ1 = 1.92 ns) and slow decay coefficient (τ2 = 7.86 ns), corresponding to two luminescent centers. The ratio of two components, calculated from weighting C1 and C2, represents the lifetime weighted fractional intensity of the two-exponential fits. With the addition of QCT, the fast component τ1 weight decreases from 47.77% to 36.93% and the τ2 weight increases from 52.23% to 63.07%. Besides, the average fluorescence lifetime of N, Cu-CDs with QCT is about 4.81 ns that is shorter than that of N, Cu-CDs (5.02 ns), further verifying the transfer of electrons form the donor N, Cu-CDs to the acceptor QCT [43]. Simultaneously, fluorescence decay curve of N, Cu-CDs in the presence and absence of QCT at the excitation wavelength of 450 nm is also acquired in Fig. 5(i). Similarly, the increase of slow decay coefficient (from 65.15% to 77.14%) and the decrease of average fluorescence lifetime (from 5.70 ns to 5.31 ns) at higher excitation also illustrate the ET process between N, Cu-CDs and QCT.

To evaluate the anti-interference capability of the designed method for two analytes, the fluorescence responses of N, Cu-CDs towards various metal ions and amino acids are recorded, including Na+, K+, Ag+, Hg2+, Cu2+, Mg2+, Cd2+, Pb2+, Fe3+, Al3+, Cys, Ala, Pro, Ser, Asn, Trp and Leu, as shown in Fig. S14. There is no evident influence on the fluorescence response of N, Cu-CDs towards two analytes, implying that N, Cu-CDs have excellent detection performances and can be employed under complex ion coexisting conditions. Meanwhile, the performance of the proposed assays for the detection of DOX and QCT are compared with the other works in Table S1, reflecting the improvement of fluorescence probe.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Dual-channel fluorescence sensing platform based on μPADs
ce:section.ce:para: Although fluorescent detection can satisfy the requirement of quantitative analysis of analyte, the analyses are often limited to standard test instruments and dark environment conditions. Thereby, developing a portable and integrated device is vital for real-time detection. Herein, a dual-channel fluorescence sensing platform is designed, which consists of three main parts: light source, test window and μPADs. Dual-channel mode is constructed based on two kinds of mini lamps with a wavelength of 365 nm and 410 nm, respectively. The device structure and light path are presented in Fig. 6 (a), where the mirror is equipped to change the way that light moves for accurate excitation. The refracted light falls on μPADs containing N, Cu-CDs and analytes, and then, the images can be observed from the test windows (Fig. 6(b)). The μPADs are fabricated by non-fluorescent filter paper with a square of 48 mm × 17 mm size. It contains a central sample zone (diameter, 11 mm) and two equal test zones (diameter, 11 mm), which are connected via I-shaped path (width, 7 mm; length, 8 mm). The sample entrances central sample zone and splits into two test zones along with I-shaped path for simultaneous sensing targets (Fig. 6(c)). To carry out the detection, the two reaction zones are immersed into N, Cu-CDs solution and dried at room temperature to form fluorescent paper zones.

As shown in Fig. 6(d), the fluorescence zones produce blue at 365 nm and 410 nm excitation, respectively. After adding DOX/QCT, analytes infiltrate through two parallel hydrophilic channels to reaction zones and react with N, Cu-CDs. The appearance of μPADs in sunlight has no obvious change, while the fluorescence intensities of two zones quench with different degrees, which promotes the realization of visual detection (Fig. S15). Considering the data processing capacity of the smartphone, app software is applied for obtaining the corresponding RGB values of these photographs through collecting photo images. The relationship between B values and concentration of DOX is presented in Fig. 6(e), where a linear range of 0–50 μM is obtained and the detection limit is calculated to be 81.6 nM. Different from the direct quenching of two test zones, color change from dark blue to cyan of test zone under 410 nm light is presented due to the appearance of FA-2 after QCT adding. The ratio value of B/G is decreased along with the QCT concentration increase and a linear range of 0–100 μM is obtained with the limit detection of 75.4 nM (Fig. 6(f)). Therefore, these results prove that the developed sensing platform not only presents excellent performance for real-time detection of DOX or QCT, but also has considerable potential for the rapid discrimination of them.

DOX and QCT, as common feed additives, are easily enriched in food product, especially in meat product, and accumulates through the food chain causing potential threats to human health. Thus, 100 μM of QCT is spiked to the meat leachate for detection (Fig. S16). The results show that it is feasible to distinguish and detect DOX or QCT in food using dual-channel fluorescence sensing platform based on μPADs. Besides, milk, tap water and waste water are selected to verify the practicability of the sensing probe in real samples. The corresponding images are presented in Fig. 6(g) and the analysis of t-test is implemented when HPLC is chosen as a reference method (Table S2). The recovery rate of fluorescence detection method ranges from 96.4% to 107.4% with a low RSD of 2.84%–8.71%, indicating a good accuracy of the developed method. Moreover, the t-test shows no significant differences between both methods at a 95% confidence level. All results prove that dual-channel fluorescence sensing platform based on μPADs is feasible for visual differentiation and quantitative detection of DOX and QCT.

In summary, we have developed an effective fluorescence probe based on N, Cu-CDs for the quantitative determination and visual discrimination of TCs (DOX is chosen as the typical model for detecting TCs) and QCT. Based on different ET mechanisms between N, Cu-CDs and targets, diverse detection modes are proposed with high selectivity and low LODs. The effect of IFE between DOX and N, Cu-CDs causes the fluorescence quenching under the excited wavelength of 362 nm. The result of detection shows a linear relationship in the range of 5–100 μM with the LOD of 23.8 nM calculated by single fluorescence signal. However, in the presence of QCT, besides the commonplace emission centered at (362 nm, 432 nm) in blue fluorescence region, another center at (410 nm, 490 nm) is appeared due to ET effect, which construct the basis of visual distinction between DOX and QCT by 3D ratiometric fluorescence probe. This new method shows a good linear relationship in the range of 10–100 μM and the LOD is determined to be 59.3 nM. Furthermore, a novel kind of dual-channel fluorescence sensing platform based on μPADs is designed for convenient detection and rapid detection of DOX and QCT in real samples. Through the analysis of RGB values, the sensing platform has low detection limit of 81.6 nM and 75.4 nM and achieves visual discrimination for DOX and QCT, which open a gateway to the development of a convenient and reliable method for food safety monitoring and environmental pollution analysis in the future.

Tingting Li: Analyzed experimental results, Wrote the manuscript. Guoqiang Guo: Involved in analyzing experimental results. Haoming Xing: Assisted in analyzing experimental results, Software. Yiru Wang: Assisted in analyzing experimental results, Software. Xinyu Luo: Helped in characterizations, Formal analysis. Linfan Wang: Helped in characterizations, Formal analysis. Chenjie Gu: Assisted in reviewing the manuscript. Yafei Hou: Assisted in reviewing the manuscript. Da Chen: Conducted the experiments, Revised the manuscript, Funding acquisition, All authors read and contributed to the manuscript.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported by projects from the National Natural Science Foundation of China under Grant (No. 61604084), the K. C. Wong Magna Fund in Ningbo University. The authors declare no competing ﬁnancial interest.
